meta.title: Ancient Mesopotamia
meta.author: Susan Lennox
meta.focusQuestion: What were the important features of ancient Mesopotamia?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: Human beings have been around for a long time. For much of that time, they lived in groups of families or tribes. It took a while for them to form civilized societies. A civilized society is one with rules that help people live and work together. One of the earliest civilized societies was found in ancient Mesopotamia. Mesopotamia was located where Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait are now. This "land between two rivers" was the place where civilization was born.

sections.title: The Ubaid
paragraphs: Ancient Mesopotamian civilization started between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers. That land came to be known as the Fertile Crescent. The first people to live in Mesopotamia settled the southern part of that region. Today, this area is Iraq and Kuwait.

In modern times, ancient stone tools, bricks, and pottery were found near the Euphrates River. These items told stories about the lives of the early people. Researchers learned that farming settlements began there as early as 5000 bce. They named these early settlers the Ubaid after the site where the items were discovered.

The Ubaid were the first people to settle the land known as Sumer. Before the Ubaid formed settlements, they were nomadic hunters and gatherers. They roamed from place to place in search of food and water.

In time, the Ubaid found ways to live in one place. They drained marshes and grew crops in the rich soil. They built canals to bring water to drier areas. They made pottery from river clay. In addition, they wove baskets from plant stems that grew in shallow water.

The river did not only meet their personal needs. It also gave the Ubaid goods to trade with others. Trade routes from Asia and Europe went through the rich plains of Sumeria. Traders provided items that the Ubaid could not grow or make themselves.

sections.title: Uruk, the First City
paragraphs: Over the years, there were more settlements in the Fertile Crescent. Some grew to become true cities. A number became city-states. The most important of these was Uruk (modern-day Warka, Iraq). At one point, Uruk had up to eighty thousand people. The city covered more than 2.6 square kilometers (1 sq. mi.) and had large buildings made of mud bricks. A huge ziggurat, or temple, was a center of worship.

Uruk was where Mesopotamian pictographs first appeared. Pictographs are symbols used in writing. This early written language grew out of Uruk's role as a center of trade and business. Simple images were marked on clay tablets to add up workers' pay and as a record of goods. Over time, pictographs changed into a system of writing known as cuneiform. Cuneiform symbols stood for sounds rather than objects. These sound symbols could be used to write in any language.

This phonologic system had several advantages over a pictographic system. Since the same set of symbols could be shuffled to form different words, fewer symbols were needed. The system also made it easier to express abstract concepts that would be difficult to show as pictures.

Clay tablets marked with cuneiform symbols gave information about Sumerian life during that time. The people of Uruk and other city-states lived in organized societies. Women had many of the same rights as men. At the highest level of society was a priest-king. That person led others, such as a leader of the plow, leader of the law, and so on. City rulers used trade to show how important they were. Having rare and valuable items was a way to show off their power.

One of the most famous rulers was King Gilgamesh (Bilgames in Sumerian). Gilgamesh is believed to have ruled Uruk around 2700 bce. He became an epic hero of song and legend. Poems were written about his 126-year rule. He was said to be a demigod, the son of a human king and a Sumerian mother-goddess. Gilgamesh is known for building the huge wall that protected Uruk. That wall still stands today.

In 2003, German scientists found a tomb in a Euphrates riverbed that they thought belonged to Gilgamesh. It was similar to the tomb described in the epic. The Epic of Gilgamesh is a collection of ancient Mesopotamian poems. It tells of the exciting adventures of an ancient king's battles and victories. It is thought to be the oldest epic in history.

sections.title: Warring City-States
paragraphs: As the Sumerian city-states grew and thrived, they began to fight with each other for control of the region. War became more and more common.

The earliest detailed record of Sumerian warfare shows a battle between the city-states of Lagash and Umma in 2525 bce. Figures on an upright stone post called a stele (STEE-lee) show soldiers carrying swords, spears, and shields into battle. Trade routes supplied the materials needed to craft the tools of war. Blacksmiths had discovered that two soft metals, copper and tin, could be combined to make a harder substance. This metal, known as bronze, was used in swords and spear tips. Tin, however, was rare. It could only be gotten from central Asian or European traders. Blocking trade routes was a way to keep weapons out of the hands of an opposing army.

The Sumerians' constant fighting stopped when King Sargon of Akkad (in southern Mesopotamia) took control of the region in about 2340 bce. He united the city-states into the Akkadian Empire. Sargon kept order by placing key trusted allies in positions of power. The Akkadian empire lasted until 2150 bce. At its peak, it stretched from the Mediterranean Sea to Arabia and from Iran to what is now Turkey.

The Akkadians did not speak Sumerian. However, they used Sumerian cuneiform script to write in their own language. The earliest Sumerian writings in which the author was known were poems by Sargon's daughter Enheduanna. She served as high priestess in the city of Ur.

sections.title: Prosperity and Advancement
paragraphs: Peace during the Akkadian empire brought progress to Mesopotamia. Under Akkadian rule, Mesopotamia created the first mail system. Clay tablets were marked with cuneiform messages, then wrapped in clay envelopes. An envelope was pressed with the seal of the sender and the name and address of the receiver. The clay envelope had to be broken in order to get to the message inside.

A central government carried out laws and regulations. Trade grew and expanded. Business deals used cylinder seals—carved tubes that were pressed into clay. Pressing one's seal into the clay was like signing one's name today.

Around 3500 bce, the Sumerians began using a round flat mechanical device to make clay bowls. The potter's wheel sat horizontally on an axis and, when spun, allowed the potter to make a bowl with even edges and surfaces. After much experimenting, the Sumerians tried attaching one wheel to each side of a single axle. The result made it easy to carry loads. Other civilizations copied the Sumerians' invention, and wheels were soon used across the world.

The Mesopotamians also developed the idea of numerical place value. The way we measure time uses this same system from long ago. We use a system created by the ancient Mesopotamians—base sixty. There are sixty seconds in one minute and sixty minutes in one hour.

sections.title: Fall of an Empire
paragraphs: The Akkadian Empire ended in about 2150 bce after many revolts and an invasion by the Gutians (GOO-tee-uhnz). The Gutians came from the mountains to the north and held regular raids on the outer parts of the kingdom. This made it unsafe for Akkadians to travel or work in the fields. In time, the Gutian nomads took over Akkad, and the Akkadian Empire collapsed.

The Gutians let the canals fall apart and brought on a terrible famine. Their 125-year rule of Sumer led to what came to be known as the Dark Age of Mesopotamia. It was not until the seventeenth century bce that Mesopotamia would return as a thriving civilization with an empire called Babylonia.

vocabulary.definition: existing as or relating to an idea rather than a physical thing; not concrete
vocabulary.sentence: The system also made it easier to express abstract concepts that would be difficult to show as pictures.

options: relating to an idea rather than a physical thing

vocabulary.definition: cities or urban areas that act as independent countries

options: cities that act as independent countries

vocabulary.definition: having an advanced level of social order and cultural development
vocabulary.sentence: A civilized society is one with rules that help people live and work together.

options: having an advanced level of social order and cultural development

vocabulary.definition: a system of writing made up of wedge-shaped characters, used in parts of the ancient Middle East
vocabulary.sentence: Over time, pictographs changed into a system of writing known as cuneiform.

options: a system of writing with wedge-shaped characters

vocabulary.definition: a being from mythology who is part human and part god
vocabulary.sentence: He was said to be a demigod, the son of a human king and a Sumerian mother-goddess.

options: a being who is part human and part god

vocabulary.definition: a collection of nations or people ruled by one person or government
vocabulary.sentence: He united the city-states into the Akkadian Empire.

options: a collection of nations or people ruled by one person or government

vocabulary.definition: moving from place to place without a permanent home
vocabulary.sentence: Before the Ubaid formed settlements, they were nomadic hunters and gatherers.

options: moving from place to place without a permanent home

vocabulary.definition: of or relating to the study of speech sounds within or among languages
vocabulary.sentence: This phonologic system had several advantages over a pictographic system.

options: speech sounds within or among languages

vocabulary.definition: symbols or pictures that represent words or ideas
vocabulary.sentence: Pictographs are symbols used in writing.

options: symbols or pictures that represent words or ideas

vocabulary.sentence: The Gutians came from the mountains to the north and held regular raids on the outer parts of the kingdom.

vocabulary.definition: a large, upright slab or column of rock, usually inscribed with designs or words
vocabulary.sentence: Figures on an upright stone post called a stele show soldiers carrying swords, spears, and shields into battle.

options: a large, upright stone slab with inscribed designs

vocabulary.definition: a rectangular structure in ancient Mesopotamia with steps up the sides and a temple at the top
vocabulary.sentence: A huge ziggurat, or temple, was a center of worship.

options: a rectangular structure with steps and a temple at the top

questions.question: What was Mesopotamia, and where was it located?
questions.evidence: Mesopotamia was located where Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait are now. This "land between two rivers" was the place where civilization was born.
choices: The land between two rivers, located where Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait are now.
An island in the Mediterranean Sea.
A mountain region in northern Europe.
questions.explanation: Mesopotamia means "land between two rivers" — the Tigris and Euphrates — in the area of modern-day Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Kuwait.

questions.question: How were the Ubaid different from traditional nomadic people?
questions.evidence: Before the Ubaid formed settlements, they were nomadic hunters and gatherers. They roamed from place to place in search of food and water. In time, the Ubaid found ways to live in one place.
choices: The Ubaid settled in one place, drained marshes, grew crops, and built canals instead of roaming.
The Ubaid continued to roam but traded more.
The Ubaid moved to the mountains.
The Ubaid gave up farming entirely.
questions.explanation: Unlike nomadic people who moved around, the Ubaid settled down, drained marshes, grew crops, built canals, and made pottery.

questions.question: What made Uruk the most important city-state?
questions.evidence: The most important of these was Uruk. At one point, Uruk had up to eighty thousand people. The city covered more than 2.6 square kilometers and had large buildings made of mud bricks. A huge ziggurat, or temple, was a center of worship.
choices: It had 80,000 people, large buildings, a ziggurat, and was where pictographs and cuneiform first appeared.
It was located far from rivers.
questions.explanation: Uruk was the largest and most important city-state, with 80,000 people, a ziggurat, and it was where writing (pictographs and cuneiform) first appeared.

questions.question: What was cuneiform, and how did it change Mesopotamian culture?
questions.evidence: Over time, pictographs changed into a system of writing known as cuneiform. Cuneiform symbols stood for sounds rather than objects. These sound symbols could be used to write in any language.
choices: A writing system where symbols stood for sounds rather than objects, allowing people to write in any language.
A system of counting trade goods.
questions.explanation: Cuneiform was a writing system with wedge-shaped symbols that stood for sounds, making it possible to write in any language — a huge advance over pictographs.

questions.question: In what ways did trade influence the culture of ancient Mesopotamia?
questions.evidence: Trade routes from Asia and Europe went through the rich plains of Sumeria. Traders provided items that the Ubaid could not grow or make themselves. Trade routes supplied the materials needed to craft the tools of war.
questions.explanation: Trade brought new materials (copper, tin for bronze), ideas, and wealth. Trade routes also became sources of conflict — blocking them was a military strategy.
opinionPrompts: How did trade affect both cooperation and warfare?
What materials came through trade routes?

questions.question: Why was Mesopotamia also known as the Fertile Crescent?
questions.evidence: Ancient Mesopotamian civilization started between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers. That land came to be known as the Fertile Crescent.
questions.explanation: The land between the Tigris and Euphrates Rivers had rich soil that supported farming, making it 'fertile' — and the arc-shaped area on a map resembled a crescent.
opinionPrompts: What features made the land fertile?
How did the rivers help people settle there?

questions.question: How did ancient Mesopotamia influence the world?
questions.evidence: The Mesopotamians also developed the idea of numerical place value. The way we measure time uses this same system from long ago. We use a system created by the ancient Mesopotamians—base sixty.
questions.explanation: Mesopotamia gave the world writing (cuneiform), the wheel, the potter's wheel, the first mail system, base-60 number system (our 60-second minutes), and the concept of city-states.
opinionPrompts: What inventions from Mesopotamia do we still use today?
How did their writing system influence later cultures?

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: The Ubaid were the first settlers of Mesopotamia who transitioned from nomadic life to farming settlements and trade.
keyDetails: They were the first to settle the land known as Sumer
They drained marshes, grew crops, and built canals to irrigate drier areas
They made pottery and wove baskets from river materials
Trade routes from Asia and Europe brought goods they couldn't produce themselves
distractors: They lived in large cities with ziggurats

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Uruk was the most important city-state in Mesopotamia, where writing and organized society first developed.
keyDetails: Uruk had up to 80,000 people with large mud-brick buildings and a ziggurat
Pictographs appeared as symbols used in writing for trade records
Cuneiform replaced pictographs — symbols stood for sounds, not objects
King Gilgamesh became an epic hero and built the wall protecting Uruk

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: City-states fought for control until King Sargon united them into the Akkadian Empire.
keyDetails: City-states fought each other for control, making war common
Bronze weapons were made from copper and tin brought through trade routes
King Sargon of Akkad united the city-states into an empire in about 2340 bce
The empire stretched from the Mediterranean to Arabia at its peak
distractors: The empire lasted until modern times

summarizeActivities.mainIdea: Peace under the Akkadian Empire led to major innovations that influenced the modern world.
keyDetails: The first mail system used clay tablets in sealed clay envelopes
The potter's wheel led to the invention of wheeled carts for transport
They developed numerical place value and base-60 system
We still use base-60 today: 60 seconds in a minute, 60 minutes in an hour
distractors: The mail system used paper letters

intro.title: Civilized
intro.content: Having rules and social order that help people live and work together. Not just living in families or tribes!

intro.title: Summarize
intro.content: A summary tells the MOST IMPORTANT details: Who? What? When? Where? Why? Leave out the small stuff!

ubaid.title: Nomadic
ubaid.content: Moving from place to place without a permanent home. The Ubaid STOPPED being nomadic when they settled down!

uruk.title: Ziggurat
uruk.content: A big stepped temple in Mesopotamia. Like a pyramid with steps on the outside and a temple on top!

uruk.title: Cuneiform
uruk.content: Wedge-shaped writing symbols. It replaced pictographs and could write ANY language because symbols stood for SOUNDS!

uruk.title: Adjectives
uruk.content: 'rich plains of Sumeria' — 'rich' is an adjective describing the noun 'plains'. Adjectives tell which one, how many, or what kind!

warring.title: Stele
warring.content: A tall stone slab with carved designs. The Stele of the Vultures shows the earliest record of Sumerian warfare!

warring.title: Empire
warring.content: Many nations or people ruled by ONE government. Sargon created the first empire by uniting all the city-states.

prosperity.title: Adjectives
prosperity.content: 'first mail system' — 'first' is an adjective telling which one. Find adjectives on pages 8-11!

prosperity.title: Abstract
prosperity.content: Relating to ideas, not physical things. Numbers and language are abstract concepts — you can't touch them!

fall.title: Raids
fall.content: Sudden surprise attacks. The Gutians raided the kingdom, making it unsafe to travel or farm.
